content:
# Genesis Chapter 20 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11r–11v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_020_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_020_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 15–16 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** From there Abraham journeyed toward the territory of the Negeb and settled between Kadesh and Shur. And he sojourned in Gerar.

**2** And Abraham said of Sarah his wife, "She is my sister." And Abimelech king of Gerar sent and took Sarah.

**3** But God came to Abimelech in a dream by night and said to him, "Behold, you are a dead man because of the woman whom you have taken, for she is a man's wife."

**4** Now Abimelech had not approached her. So he said, "Lord, will you kill a nation that is righteous?

**5** Did he not himself say to me, 'She is my sister'? And she herself said, 'He is my brother.' In the integrity of my heart and the innocence of my hands I have done this."

**6** Then God said to him in the dream, "Yes, I know that you have done this in the integrity of your heart, and it was I who kept you from sinning against me. Therefore I did not let you touch her.

**7** Now then, return the man's wife, for he is a prophet, and he will pray for you, and you shall live. But if you do not return her, know that you shall surely die, you and all who are yours."

**8** So Abimelech rose early in the morning and called all his servants and told all these things in their hearing, and the men were very afraid.

**9** Then Abimelech called Abraham and said to him, "What have you done to us? And how have I sinned against you, that you have brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin? You have done to me things that ought not to be done."

**10** And Abimelech said to Abraham, "What did you see, that you did this thing?"

**11** Abraham said, "I did it because I thought, 'There is no fear of God at all in this place, and they will kill me because of my wife.'

**12** Besides, she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father though not the daughter of my mother, and she became my wife.

**13** And when God caused me to wander from my father's house, I said to her, 'This is the kindness you must do me: at every place to which we come, say of me, "He is my brother."'"

**14** Then Abimelech took sheep and oxen, and male servants and female servants, and gave them to Abraham, and returned Sarah his wife to him.

**15** And Abimelech said, "Behold, my land is before you; dwell where it pleases you."

**16** To Sarah he said, "Behold, I have given your brother a thousand pieces of silver. It is a covering of the eyes for you before all who are with you, and before everyone you are vindicated."

**17** Then Abraham prayed to God, and God healed Abimelech, and also healed his wife and his female servants so that they bore children.

**18** For the LORD had closed fast every womb of the house of Abimelech because of Sarah, Abraham's wife.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 20 spans Folios 11r–11v.

   **Folio 11r** (GEN_020_page_001.jpg): This folio is the recto of Folio 11, which carried the end of Genesis 19 in its right-hand columns (the destruction narrative, the cave episode, and the etiological conclusions about Moab and Ben-ammi in vv. 37–38). Genesis 20 opens in the left-hand columns of this same folio, beginning with Abraham's journey southward to the Negeb — וַיִּסַּע מִשָּׁם אַבְרָהָם ("and Abraham journeyed from there"). The characteristic three-column layout of the Leningrad Codex is clearly visible, with the Masoretic pointing, cantillation marks (te'amim), and parasha notation legible. The column carrying Genesis 20:1 begins in the standard square Hebrew script of the Ben Asher tradition. Verses 1 through approximately verse 13 are visible on this folio before the column break leads to the verso.

   **Folio 11v** (GEN_020_page_002.jpg): The verso of Folio 11 carries the remainder of Genesis 20 — from approximately verse 14 through verse 18 — followed immediately by the opening of Genesis 21 (the birth of Isaac) in the subsequent columns. The chapter conclusion is clearly legible: the healing prayer of Abraham (v. 17), and the closing explanatory note about the LORD having closed the wombs of Abimelech's household because of Sarah (v. 18). The transition to Genesis 21:1 ("The LORD visited Sarah as he had said") is visible in the column that follows.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 15 (GEN_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the Genesis 19–20 transition area, with the Genesis 20 narrative visible in the three-column uncial layout of Vaticanus. The chapter marker "CAP. XXII" is visible in the left margin (Vaticanus uses its own sequential chapter enumeration, which does not correspond to the standard modern chapter numbering). Page 16 (GEN_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the conclusion of Genesis 20 and the opening of Genesis 21, with the chapter marker "CAP. XXIII" visible at the top, indicating the next Vaticanus section division. The 4th-century uncial hand is consistent throughout.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. וַיִּסַּע מִשָּׁם — "From There Abraham Journeyed" (v. 1) — Geographic Transition**

The chapter opens with a terse geographic notice:

וַיִּסַּע מִשָּׁם אַבְרָהָם אַרְצָה הַנֶּגֶב
(vayyissa' misham Avraham artsah hannegev)
"From there Abraham journeyed toward the territory of the Negeb."

The verb נָסַע (nasa', "to journey, to pull up stakes, to set out") is the standard patriarchal travel verb — used throughout the Abrahamic narrative for the major movements of his household (Genesis 12:9; 13:11; etc.). The phrase "from there" (מִשָּׁם, misham) anchors the departure to the previous narrative location: the vicinity of Mamre / Hebron, from which Abraham had watched the smoke of Sodom rise in Genesis 19:28. The chapter does not explain why Abraham moved. The narrative resumes as if the reader already follows the patriarch's trajectory.

The Negeb (הַנֶּגֶב, hannegev) is the semi-arid southern region of Canaan — "the South" in Hebrew (the word derives from a root meaning "dry, parched"). The formal translation renders "the territory of the Negeb" to capture the directional force of the Hebrew אַרְצָה (artsah, "toward the land/territory of").

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ἐκεῖθεν δὲ ἀπῆρεν Αβρααμ εἰς γῆν πρὸς Νοτον (ekeithen de aperen Abraam eis gen pros Noton, "and from there Abraham departed into the land toward the South") — rendering הַנֶּגֶב (hannegev) as πρὸς Νοτον ("toward the South"), a geographic translation rather than a transliteration.

**2. גְּרָר — Gerar (v. 1) — The Location**

אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מֶלֶךְ גְּרָר (Avimelekh melekh Gerar, "Abimelech king of Gerar") — Gerar is identified in the ancient sources as a city in the southern coastal plain of Canaan, in the region between the Negeb and the Mediterranean (cf. Genesis 10:19). The Philistine association of Gerar in Genesis 26 (where Isaac repeats this same episode) has led to much scholarly discussion, since the Philistines are generally considered a 12th-century BCE arrival in Canaan while the patriarchal period is set centuries earlier. The formal translation retains "Gerar" as the place name without comment on the historical question, which lies beyond the scope of translation decisions.

The name אֲבִימֶלֶךְ (Avimelekh, "Abimelech") means "my father is king" — a Canaanite/Philistine royal title that recurs across several generations in the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 20, 21, 26) and in Judges (Abimelech son of Gideon). Whether all these references are to the same person or to different rulers sharing a dynastic title is disputed.

**3. בַּחֲלוֹם הַלַּיְלָה — "In a Dream by Night" (v. 3) — Divine Communication to a Non-Israelite**

God's approach to Abimelech — בַּחֲלוֹם הַלַּיְלָה (bachalon hallailah, "in a dream by night") — is theologically remarkable. Abimelech is a Canaanite king, not a member of the Abrahamic covenant. Yet God communicates directly with him, warning him, engaging his plea of innocence, and acknowledging his integrity. The dream communication is consistent with how God speaks to foreign kings and figures throughout the Hebrew Bible (Pharaoh in Genesis 41; Nebuchadnezzar in Daniel 2; etc.).

The divine declaration in verse 3 — "Behold, you are a dead man" (הִנְּךָ מֵת, hinnekha met, literally "behold you [are] dead") — is the standard Hebrew idiom for a sentence of death, a judgment already pronounced even if not yet executed. The perfect verb form מֵת (met, "dead") expresses the certainty of the outcome as if already accomplished.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ἰδοὺ σὺ ἀποθνῄσκεις (idou su apothneskeis, "behold you are dying") — using the Greek present tense ἀποθνῄσκεις ("you are dying") rather than the perfect, which slightly differs in force from the Hebrew's stark "you are [a] dead [man]."

**4. גּוֹי צַדִּיק — "A Nation That Is Righteous" (v. 4) — Abimelech's Plea**

Abimelech's response to the divine death-sentence is immediate:

הַאַף גּוֹי צַדִּיק תַּהֲרֹג
(haf goy tsaddiq taharog)
"Will you also kill a nation that is righteous?"

The word גּוֹי (goy, "nation, people") is usually used in contrast to Israel, though here it simply denotes the people under Abimelech's rule. The adjective צַדִּיק (tsaddiq, "righteous, just, in the right") echoes Abraham's great intercession for Sodom in Genesis 18:23–25 — "Will you indeed sweep away the righteous (tsaddiq) with the wicked?" Abimelech is now making a structurally identical argument: will God destroy someone who is innocent? The verbal echo is likely deliberate on the narrator's part, inviting the reader to compare the two intercessions.

Abimelech's plea rests on two claims of innocence (v. 5): (1) Abraham himself represented Sarah as his sister; (2) Sarah herself confirmed the claim. The Hebrew formula — "in the integrity of my heart and the innocence of my hands" (בְּתָם לְבָבִי וּבְנִקְיֹן כַּפַּי, betom levavi uvnikyyon kappay) — is a double assertion of moral clean-handedness that will recur in the Psalter (cf. Psalm 26:6; 73:13). The formal translation renders both phrases.

**5. חָשַׂכְתִּי — "I Kept You" (v. 6) — Divine Moral Restraint**

God's reply to Abimelech in verse 6 is one of the most theologically precise statements in Genesis 20:

גַּם אָנֹכִי יָדַעְתִּי כִּי בְתָם לְבָבְךָ עָשִׂיתָ זֹּאת
וָאֶחְשֹׂךְ גַּם אָנֹכִי אוֹתְךָ מֵחֲטוֹ לִי
עַל כֵּן לֹא נְתַתִּיךָ לִנְגֹּעַ אֵלֶיהָ
(gam anokhi yada'ti ki betom levavkha asita zot
va'echsokh gam anokhi otkha mechato li
al ken lo netatikha lignoa' eleiha)
"Yes, I know that you have done this in the integrity of your heart,
and it was I who kept you from sinning against me.
Therefore I did not let you touch her."

The verb חָשַׂךְ (chasak, "to hold back, to keep, to restrain") describes an act of divine moral protection extended to Abimelech — a Canaanite king — preventing him from committing an unwitting sin. This is a theologically significant claim: God is presented as actively preserving the moral innocence of a non-Israelite by withholding his approach to Sarah. The implication is that had Abimelech touched Sarah, he would have sinned against God, even unknowingly — and God spared him this. The formal translation renders "it was I who kept you" to preserve the emphatic position of אָנֹכִי (anokhi, "I") in the Hebrew — literally, "also I, I kept you" — emphasizing divine agency.

The phrase לִנְגֹּעַ אֵלֶיהָ (lignoa' eleiha, "to touch her") uses the verb נָגַע (naga', "to touch, to reach, to come near") as a euphemism for sexual contact — exactly as in Genesis 26:29 and, notably, in Proverbs 6:29 ("whoever touches [naga'] his neighbor's wife will not go unpunished"). The formal translation renders "touch her" as the standard English equivalent of this idiom.

**6. נָבִיא — "He Is a Prophet" (v. 7) — Abraham Called a Prophet**

Verse 7 contains one of the most striking designations in the Abraham narrative:

כִּי נָבִיא הוּא
(ki navi hu)
"For he is a prophet."

This is the only time in Genesis that Abraham is called a נָבִיא (navi', "prophet"). The word נָבִיא is the standard Hebrew term for a prophet — one who speaks on behalf of God, who intercedes, who stands in the divine council. The application to Abraham here is theologically loaded: it establishes his intercessory capacity ("he will pray for you, and you shall live") while also signaling that Abraham's role in the narrative goes beyond that of patriarch and covenant-bearer. He is an intercessor — a prophetic mediator between God and other peoples.

The verb יִתְפַּלֵּל (yitpallel, "he will pray") is the Hithpael of פָּלַל (palal, "to pray, to intercede"), the standard Hebrew verb for prayer and intercession. The prayer of Abraham for Abimelech's household in verse 17 will fulfill this divine declaration. This is one of the earliest instances in the Hebrew Bible of intercessory prayer as a formal function of a named individual — and it is explicitly authorized by God in a divine speech.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ὅτι προφήτης ἐστίν (hoti prophetes estin, "for he is a prophet") — using the Greek word προφήτης (prophetes, "prophet"), in exact correspondence to the Hebrew navi'. The LXX agrees with the MT.

**7. בְּתָם לְבָבִי — "The Integrity of My Heart" (vv. 5–6) — Moral Vocabulary**

The phrase בְּתָם לְבָבִי (betom levavi, "in the integrity/wholeness of my heart") appears in both Abimelech's plea (v. 5) and in God's acknowledgment (v. 6). The root תָּמַם (tamam, "to be complete, whole, without blemish") generates the noun תֹּם (tom, "completeness, integrity, blamelessness") — used throughout the Hebrew Bible for moral wholeness or uprightness (cf. Psalm 78:72, of David: "with the integrity of his heart"). The formal translation renders "integrity of my heart" in both occurrences to preserve the verbal echo.

**8. הִתְעָה אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים — "When God Caused Me to Wander" (v. 13) — The Plural Verb**

Verse 13 contains the grammatically remarkable phrase:

כַּאֲשֶׁר הִתְעוּ אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים מִבֵּית אָבִי
(ka'asher hit'u oti Elohim mibbeit avi)
"when God caused me to wander from my father's house"

The verb הִתְעוּ (hit'u) is a Hiphil plural perfect of תָּעָה (ta'ah, "to wander, to cause to stray") — and critically, it is a **plural** verb form while its subject, אֱלֹהִים (Elohim, "God"), is grammatically plural in form but normally takes a singular verb when referring to the God of Israel. Here the plural verb is used with Elohim in a manner that departs from the standard singular construction. This grammatical irregularity has been interpreted in several ways:

- **The most natural Hebrew explanation**: The plural verb with Elohim may reflect an archaic or poetic construction in which Elohim, having a plural morphological form, occasionally takes a plural verb. This is attested elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (e.g., Genesis 35:7, where Elohim is used with a plural verb in a similar context of divine journeying — "because there God [Elohim] had revealed himself to him," using a plural verb form).
- **The theological interpretation**: Some have argued that the plural verb hints at the divine council or the fullness of divine persons. This reading is not necessary for the grammar to make sense.
- **The contextual note**: Abraham is speaking to Abimelech, a non-Israelite. His use of Elohim (the more universal term for the deity) rather than YHWH is appropriate to the diplomatic register of the conversation.

The formal translation renders "when God caused me to wander" — using the singular English "God" to render Elohim while acknowledging the unusual plural verb in this note.

**9. כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם — "A Covering of the Eyes" (v. 16) — The Obscure Idiom**

Verse 16 contains one of the most difficult phrases in Genesis 20:

הִנֵּה הוּא לָךְ כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם לְכֹל אֲשֶׁר אִתָּךְ
וְאֵת כֹּל וְנֹכָחַת
(hinneh hu lakh kesut einayim lekhol asher ittakh
ve'et kol venokhahat)
"Behold, it is a covering of the eyes for you before all who are with you,
and before everyone you are vindicated."

Abimelech's speech to Sarah in verse 16 is notoriously difficult. The phrase כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם (kesut einayim, "covering of the eyes") is an idiom that occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible, making its precise meaning uncertain. Several interpretations have been proposed:

- **"Vindication / clearing of reputation"**: The thousand pieces of silver function as a public settlement that "covers" (clears, removes) any suspicion that might rest upon Sarah's eyes — i.e., that people might have judged her guilty of willing adultery. The payment publicly establishes her innocence. On this reading, "covering of the eyes" means "something that silences the gaze of accusation" — restoring honor.
- **"Compensation / hush money"**: The phrase could mean a payment to avert the eyes — to make others look away from the scandal and say nothing. This is a less favorable reading for Abimelech.
- **"A veil"**: Some interpreters read the phrase as a reference to the veil that Abraham had asked Sarah to wear (cf. Genesis 24:65 for the significance of the veil as a marker of the veiled woman's status), with the silver being a payment for a proper veil that restores her status as a married woman.

The final clause וְנֹכָחַת (venokhahat) is also contested: the verb נָכַח (nakhach, "to be straight, right, in front of") in the Niphal can mean "to be proved right, to be vindicated." The formal translation renders "you are vindicated" as the most likely sense in context.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ταῦτα ἔσται σοι εἰς τιμὴν τοῦ προσώπου σου (tauta estai soi eis timen tou prosopou sou, "these will be for you as honor for your face") — interpreting the phrase as an honorific payment restoring Sarah's dignity and public standing. The LXX's interpretive rendering supports the "vindication / honor restoration" reading.

The formal translation follows the MT's specific idiom "covering of the eyes" as the primary rendering, with "you are vindicated" for the closing clause, while noting the uncertainty in this note.

**10. וַיַּעְצֹר יְהוָה — "The LORD Had Closed Fast" (v. 18) — YHWH in an Elohim Chapter**

The chapter's final verse uses YHWH (the divine personal name) rather than Elohim, which is the predominant divine name throughout Genesis 20:

כִּי עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה בְּעַד כָּל רֶחֶם לְבֵית אֲבִימֶלֶךְ
(ki atsor atsar YHWH be'ad kol rechem lebet Avimelekh)
"For the LORD had closed fast every womb of the house of Abimelech."

The verb עָצַר (atsar, "to shut, to hold back, to restrain") is used in the emphatic absolute infinitive construction — עָצֹר עָצַר (atsor atsar, "had completely shut / shut fast") — expressing thorough, complete closure. The phrase בְּעַד כָּל רֶחֶם (be'ad kol rechem, "over/against every womb") indicates that the sterility was comprehensive — it affected every woman in Abimelech's household, not only the king's wife.

The appearance of YHWH in v. 18 (as opposed to the Elohim used throughout vv. 1–17) is noted throughout the history of interpretation. The manifest's note is accurate: the chapter is predominantly Elohistic, with YHWH appearing only in this closing verse. Some source-critical approaches use this distribution as evidence for separate composition, but the formal translation renders the text as it stands in the Leningrad Codex, using "the LORD" for YHWH and "God" for Elohim consistently.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ἔκλεισεν γὰρ κύριος ἔξωθεν πᾶσαν μήτραν ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ τοῦ Αβιμελεχ (ekleisen gar kyrios exothen pasan metran en to oiko tou Abimelech, "for the Lord had closed from outside every womb in the house of Abimelech") — using κύριος (kyrios, "Lord") for YHWH, in agreement with MT. The LXX adds the word ἔξωθεν (exothen, "from outside"), which has no equivalent in the MT.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 20

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 15–16 (GEN_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The LXX of Genesis 20 generally follows the MT closely; the following variants are of note:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 20:1 | אַרְצָה הַנֶּגֶב ("toward the territory of the Negeb") | εἰς γῆν πρὸς Νοτον ("into the land toward the South") | "the territory of the Negeb" | LXX translates הַנֶּגֶב as "the South" rather than transliterating; formal translation follows MT place-name |
| 20:3 | הִנְּךָ מֵת ("you are a dead man") | ἀποθνῄσκεις ("you are dying") | "you are a dead man" | MT's perfect form expresses certainty of death; LXX's present tense is slightly weaker in force |
| 20:5 | בְּנִקְיֹן כַּפַּי ("the innocence of my hands") | ἐν καθαρότητι χειρῶν ("in the cleanness of hands") | "the innocence of my hands" | Exact equivalence in sense; both stress Abimelech's clean-handedness |
| 20:7 | נָבִיא הוּא ("he is a prophet") | προφήτης ἐστίν ("he is a prophet") | "he is a prophet" | Exact agreement; LXX prophetes = MT navi' |
| 20:12 | בַּת אָבִי ("daughter of my father") | ἐκ πατρός μου ("from my father") | "daughter of my father" | MT preserves "daughter" explicitly; LXX slightly compresses |
| 20:13 | הִתְעוּ אֹתִי ("caused me to wander," plural verb) | ἀπήγαγέν με ("led me away," singular verb) | "caused me to wander" | LXX normalizes the unusual plural Elohim + plural verb construction to a singular, removing the grammatical ambiguity; formal translation follows MT |
| 20:16 | כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם ("covering of the eyes") | εἰς τιμὴν τοῦ προσώπου σου ("as honor for your face") | "covering of the eyes" | The most significant interpretive variant in the chapter; LXX paraphrases the obscure MT idiom as a public honorific; formal translation retains the MT idiom with explanation |
| 20:17 | וַיְּרַפֵּא אֱלֹהִים ("God healed") | ἰάσατο δὲ ὁ θεός ("and God healed") | "God healed" | Exact equivalence; LXX renders Elohim as ho theos |
| 20:18 | עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה ("the LORD had closed fast") | ἔκλεισεν... κύριος ("the Lord closed") | "the LORD had closed fast" | LXX omits the emphatic absolute infinitive construction (atsor atsar); MT's emphatic doubling is preserved in the formal translation; LXX also adds ἔξωθεν ("from outside") with no MT equivalent |

The most significant LXX variant in Genesis 20 is the rendering of כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם (kesut einayim, "covering of the eyes") in verse 16. The Vaticanus LXX paraphrases this as εἰς τιμὴν τοῦ προσώπου σου ("as honor for your face") — a free interpretive rendering that resolves the ambiguity of the Hebrew idiom by choosing the "public vindication / honor" reading. The formal translation retains the MT's literal idiom.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Second Wife-Sister Episode — A Narrative Type-Scene**

Genesis 20 is the second of three "wife-sister" episodes in Genesis:
1. **Genesis 12:10–20**: Abraham and Sarah in Egypt before Pharaoh — Sarah taken, plagues sent, Abraham sent away with wealth.
2. **Genesis 20**: Abraham and Sarah in Gerar before Abimelech — Sarah taken, divine dream warning, Abimelech restores Sarah with gifts and rebuke.
3. **Genesis 26:1–11**: Isaac and Rebekah before Abimelech in Gerar — the same king (or his successor), the same claim, but this time Abimelech discovers the truth by observation rather than divine warning and issues a protective decree.

The three episodes form a type-scene — a recurring narrative pattern in which the patriarchal couple's status is threatened in foreign territory. The theological question the pattern raises is persistent: why does Abraham repeat the same deception that got him in trouble in Genesis 12? The text does not answer this explicitly. Abraham's response in verses 11–13 offers three reasons: (1) he feared for his life due to lack of "fear of God" in Gerar; (2) the claim is technically accurate, since Sarah is his half-sister; (3) this was a standing arrangement agreed upon when they left his father's house.

**Elohim vs. YHWH — The Divine Name Question**

The consistent use of Elohim throughout Genesis 20 (vv. 3, 6, 11, 13, 17) — with the single exception of YHWH in verse 18 — has been a touchstone of source-critical analysis since the 18th century. The classic Documentary Hypothesis assigned this chapter largely to the "E" (Elohist) source on the basis of this divine name pattern, in contrast to the "J" (Yahwist) material of Genesis 12:10–20 (which uses YHWH). The formal translation makes no source-critical judgments but renders the divine names as they appear in the Leningrad Codex:
- אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) = "God" throughout vv. 1–17
- יְהוָה (YHWH) = "the LORD" in v. 18 only

**The Moral Complexity of the Chapter — Three Points of Tension**

Genesis 20 presents a more complex moral portrait than its counterpart in Genesis 12:

1. **Abimelech's righteousness**: Unlike Pharaoh in Genesis 12, Abimelech is not presented as a villain. God explicitly acknowledges his integrity (v. 6). Abimelech's conduct — his protest, his nocturnal dialogue with God, his immediate restitution with generous gifts — marks him as a person of genuine moral seriousness. The text does not present a simple Israelite-insider/Gentile-outsider polarity: the Gentile king behaves with more moral clarity than the patriarch.

2. **Abraham's deception**: Abraham's conduct in this chapter is morally ambiguous. His fear-based deception (vv. 11–13) is more rationalized here than in Genesis 12: he provides a three-part justification. Yet the text makes clear that his deception placed Abimelech in moral jeopardy — God had to intervene to prevent Abimelech from unwitting sin. Abraham's prophetic status (v. 7) and intercession (v. 17) coexist uncomfortably with his repeated resort to deception.

3. **Sarah's agency**: The text records that Sarah herself confirmed the claim — "He is my brother" (v. 5). This is the only episode in which Sarah's own participation in the deception is explicitly noted. Whether this reflects her assent to the arrangement of verse 13 or a separate speech is not clear, but it makes her an active participant rather than a passive object of exchange.

**Abraham as Intercessor (v. 17) — A Prophetic Act**

The fulfillment of God's declaration in verse 7 — "he will pray for you" — comes in verse 17 with terse economy: וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל אַבְרָהָם אֶל הָאֱלֹהִים (vayyitpallel Avraham el ha'Elohim, "Then Abraham prayed to God"). No record of the prayer's content is given. The answer is immediate: God healed Abimelech, his wife, and his female servants. The intercession of the prophet works — the healing follows the prayer as surely as the LORD's restoration of the wombs follows the return of Sarah. The closing verse (v. 18) explains the suffering that had prompted the crisis and frames the healing as its resolution.
